input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, comprising components such as the first axis, permanent magnet synchronous motor, and gear transmission. This industrial robotic system operates in a manufacturing environment requiring high precision motion control, making it suitable for digital twinning to enhance accuracy and real-time monitoring capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for system structure and behavior modeling across multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are employed for component and connector-based system descriptions. Methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are applied to manage complexity. Simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink are integrated for detailed behavior modeling, complemented by general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) and other simulation frameworks for comprehensive system analysis.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Shadow operates with a one-way data flow from physical assets to digital counterparts, utilizing real-time live measurements and streamed data traces for synchronization, where active components process incoming data to maintain temporal consistency without explicit frequency or latency specifications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Shadow Reference Model is structured as a UML class diagram defining key concepts (Asset, DataTrace, Processing) and their relationships for manufacturing applications, specifying architecture patterns and component integration for the Digital Twin constellation.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external modeling tools including MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java), while addressing heterogeneous modeling notations and stakeholder perspectives as part of MBSE processes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include structural representations via SysML and Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc for component & connector perspectives; behavioral models using state machines in SysML v1.7 and MontiArc Automaton; analytical models through tools like MathWorks Simulink for detailed simulation. Data sources consist of contextual data traces from physical systems, aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes. Data management approaches involve integrating heterogeneous modeling notations, tools (e.g., Simulink, C++/Java code), and stakeholder perspectives to maintain consistency across different abstraction levels.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor time series data is streamed from physical assets to the digital shadow via MQTT protocol as part of an automated one-way data flow. Transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, and data integration mechanisms are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is utilized for sensor data transmission; network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process for digital shadows involves structured steps including Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, and Data identification as defined in Section II.C. Section IV.A demonstrates application of this methodology for validation and evolution, ensuring systematic development through iterative refinement and testing.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system generates insights such as identification of joint friction and detection of friction loss to enhance robot control accuracy. Decision support is delivered through real-time visualization in a dashboard interface, enabling user interaction with analytical results and informed adjustments based on derived recommendations.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with industry standards including SysML for model-based systems engineering (MBSE), architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25,26], and SysML IBDs [17,27], methodologies like RFLP [18,19], OOSEM [20], SPES [21], SMArDT [22], simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink [28], and general-purpose programming languages (e.g., C++, Java). Interoperability is addressed through integration of heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives [31].

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the robot as the MABI MAX 100 with six axes and accurately lists key components like the first axis, motor, and gear transmission. It correctly identifies the operational domain as manufacturing and the need for precision. However, it misses specific behavioral details mentioned in the text, such as the 'path repeatability (0.06 mm)', 'positional accuracy (0.1 mm)', the specific 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' controller, and the 'secondary encoder' setup. Quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents focus on the *creation* and *methodology* of a Digital Shadow for friction modeling, rather than describing specific services provided by a Digital Twin (such as optimization, prediction, or control services) to end users. The text mentions visualization in a dashboard and improving motion accuracy as a result, but does not define a list of 'dt_services' with capabilities and target users. Therefore, the claim that this characteristic is not in the document is correct. Quotes: 'In this paper, we apply and refine a method to create Digital Shadows... with operational data', 'integrated visualization within a unified dashboard'.

The description is highly specific and strongly grounded in Section II.A of the source text. It accurately lists the tools and methodologies: 'Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)' with 'SysML,' Architecture Description Languages such as 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc,' methodologies like 'RFLP... OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT,' and simulation tools including 'MathWorks Simulink' and 'general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java)'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the text's definition of a Digital Shadow as an 'automated one-way data flow' and describes the system's use of 'real-time, data-driven representations' and 'live measurements'. It correctly notes that data is 'streamed via MQTT' and that 'active components to interpret and process this incoming data' are required, while accurately observing that specific synchronization frequencies or latency values are not provided in the text.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on a single digital shadow for one robot axis and does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures, or decentralized architectures.

The extraction correctly identifies the system architecture based on the 'Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram'. It accurately lists the key concepts defined in the model: 'Asset', 'DataTrace', and 'Processing', and describes their role in the system. The description of the 'Processing component' that 'consumes... input data traces and produces output data' supports the claim about component integration.

The extraction is highly specific and accurate, identifying the integration with MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools, and languages like C++ and Java within the context of MBSE. It correctly captures the text regarding coping with 'different modeling notations' and integrating 'heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives'. Supported by quotes: 'combined with further (modeling) tools such as MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java' and 'cope with models in different modeling notations'.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The text describes the modeling phases of the system (e.g., 'development phase') and the robot's operation, but it does not explicitly define the lifecycle stages, transition management, or governance approaches specific to the Digital Twin entity itself. Therefore, the extraction stating 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction accurately identifies the structured steps for creating digital shadows (Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, Data identification) and their application in Section IV.A. However, it omits the third step listed in the source: 'Use or creation of calculations or models' (Section II.C and IV.A). It correctly references the refinement and application of the methodology, though 'testing' is implied by the validation step rather than explicitly labeled as such.

The extraction correctly identifies the system's purpose: 'identification of joint friction', 'detection of friction loss', and 'improve robot control accuracy' (Section IV.A and Abstract). It correctly identifies the 'dashboard' as the visualization method (Section IV Intro). The phrase 'derived recommendations' is slightly inferential as the text states the data 'should be applied to improve the robot control’s accuracy' rather than explicitly generating recommendations for the user, but the context aligns.

The extraction provides a highly detailed and accurate list of the standards, languages, and tools mentioned in Section II.A, including 'SysML', 'RFLP', 'OOSEM', 'SPES', 'SMArDT', 'ACME', 'AADL', 'MontiArc', 'MathWorks Simulink', and 'C++ or Java'. It also correctly captures the interoperability aspect regarding 'heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives'.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source text focuses on the technical implementation of a digital shadow for robot motion control, specifically modeling joint friction. It mentions data 'traceability' regarding its origin and computation, but does not discuss data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection measures, or compliance with regulations. Therefore, the extraction 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The document describes a physical 'safety cell' restricting the robot's working range and mentions data streaming via MQTT, but it does not contain information on cybersecurity protocols, access control, authentication systems, threat mitigation, or fail-safe mechanisms for the digital twin system. Consequently, the extraction 'Not in Document' is correct.
